title: The Consolation of Israel
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: The Consolation of Israel - Luke 2_22-35 - Pastor Tito Lyro.txt
outline: The presentation of Jesus in the temple (vv.22-24)
Simeon: just, devout, waiting for the Consolation of Israel, Spirit-led (v.25)
Revelation: Simeon would not die before seeing the Lord’s Christ (v.26)
Simeon takes the baby and blesses God: the Nunc Dimittis (vv.28-32)
A light of revelation to the Gentiles and the glory of Israel
Simeon’s prophecy to Mary: this child is set for the fall and rise of many (vv.34-35)
summary: An AM Christmas/Advent sermon on Luke 2:22-35, the presentation of Jesus in the temple and Simeon’s encounter with the Messiah. Simeon—just, devout, Spirit-filled, waiting for Israel’s consolation—takes the infant Jesus and sings the Nunc Dimittis: ‘Lord, now you are letting your servant depart in peace.’ Jesus is the long-awaited consolation of Israel and a light of revelation to the Gentiles. Simeon also prophesies the inevitable division Christ will bring.
transcript:
## Introduction: The Songs of Christmas

Music and singing have been part of the Christmas celebration from the very first one. In the Gospel of Luke we have Elizabeth singing, Mary singing, the hosts of heaven singing, and now Simeon singing. Luke is the only gospel writer to record these new songs — the songs of the inception of the new covenant sealed by the blood of the Lord Jesus Christ.

Last week we considered Mary’s song, the Magnificat. This week we look at Simeon’s song, which has been known in history as the *Nunc Dimittis* — from the first two words of the Latin text, *“now dismiss.”*

The passage has four parts: the dedication of Jesus in the temple (vv. 22–24), the introduction of Simeon (vv. 25–27), his song (vv. 28–32), and his words to Mary (vv. 33–35).

## The Dedication of Jesus in the Temple (Luke 2:22–24)

Thirty-three days after the birth of a male child, the Jewish law required the parents to bring an offering to the temple. Joseph and Mary followed two separate laws in coming to the temple:

The first is the law of dedicating the firstborn, from Exodus 13, which required acknowledging that every firstborn son belonged to the Lord.

The second is the law of purification for the mother after childbirth, from Leviticus 12. The normal offering was a lamb; but for those too poor to afford a lamb, two turtledoves or two young pigeons were permitted. Joseph and Mary brought the lesser offering — which tells us that God sent His Son to be raised in a **poor but godly family**. That is a statement about God’s priorities: godliness is more important than riches in this world.

Two things about Jesus are established here. First, Jesus was the firstborn son — which means He was the oldest of other siblings. The New Testament elsewhere names at least three brothers and implies at least two sisters. The teaching that these siblings were cousins, or Joseph’s children from a previous marriage, stems from the Roman Catholic doctrine of Mary’s perpetual virginity. But the Bible clearly says Jesus was the firstborn *of the couple*, and the law reckoned firstborn status from both parents.

Second, even from His infancy, Jesus **obeyed God’s law completely** — in heart and in behavior, from the very beginning. This is of supreme importance, because God requires perfect obedience of those who would be in fellowship with Him. You and I are not that. But at the moment we believe in Jesus Christ, God counts Christ’s perfect obedience as *our* obedience. When God looks at you, He sees the obedient heart of His Son, perfectly executed on your behalf. At your worst day, if you are a believer in Jesus Christ, God looks at you and says: I love you with infinite love, because I love you through My Son.

## Simeon: Just and Devout (Luke 2:25–27)

Simeon is introduced in verses 25–27. This is the only place in the Bible where we meet him, and yet his faith and obedience gained him a place in biblical history for all generations.

What was Simeon’s claim to fame? The Holy Spirit tells us: *“This man was just and devout.”* There are not chapters written about Simeon, not books. Just those five words. What are you known for? How do you want to be remembered?

Simeon’s hope was not in his godliness but in the **Consolation of Israel** — the Messiah. He was waiting for the One whose ministry would reconcile man to God and God to man. The word *consolation* means comfort, encouragement, the calling of another alongside you. Jesus Christ is that Consolation — the One who brings man and God together.

Notice that all of this was orchestrated by the Holy Spirit. The Spirit had revealed to Simeon that he would not die before he had seen the Lord’s Christ. The Spirit brought him to the temple at the exact moment Joseph and Mary were presenting the child Jesus. This is the universal reality: **unless the Holy Spirit opens a person’s eyes to see who Jesus is, he or she will never recognize Him**.

## The Nunc Dimittis: Simeon’s Song (Luke 2:28–32)

> *“Lord, now You are letting Your servant depart in peace, according to Your word; for my eyes have seen Your salvation which You have prepared before the face of all peoples, a light to bring revelation to the Gentiles, and the glory of Your people Israel.”* (Luke 2:29–32)

Simeon had long waited for the Messiah. Now he had met Him. And meeting Jesus brought peace — not an absence of conflict, but a completeness of soul. As Psalm 62:1–2 says: *“Truly my soul silently waits for God; from Him comes my salvation. He only is my rock and my salvation.”* Simeon had found that Rock.

Simeon literally saw with his own eyes God’s salvation. And there is no salvation apart from seeing Jesus.

Notice that none of this was last-minute. Verse 31 says this salvation was *“prepared before the face of all peoples.”* Biblical history had been moving toward this moment from the fall of Adam and Eve in the garden. And the plan was not secret — it unfolded *before the face of all people*.

Simeon also declares that this Messiah is the salvation of *both* Jews and Gentiles. Some teach that Jesus came only for ethnic Israel, and when Israel rejected Him, God improvised a Plan B that gave birth to the church. But Simeon, singing under the inspiration of the Holy Spirit, declares that the plan all along was for Jesus to be the Messiah of all the nations. Jesus is the light and the glory of the world. That is why there is no other name under heaven by which a person must be saved — Jew or Gentile, whatever the ethnicity.

## Simeon’s Word to Mary (Luke 2:33–35)

> *“Behold, this Child is destined for the fall and rising of many in Israel, and for a sign which will be spoken against… yes, a sword will pierce through your own soul also, that the thoughts of many hearts may be revealed.”* (Luke 2:34–35)

Even in the midst of songs and celebration, we see an aspect of the first coming of Christ that is easy to miss: His state of humiliation. As Paul says in Philippians 2, Christ did not consider equality with God something to be grasped but humbled Himself, taking the form of a servant, becoming like us, obedient even to death on the cross. Life limited by the created order in a sin-cursed world from birth was part of what the Son had to suffer for His people.

Joseph and Mary knew Jesus was the Son of God — but there were things they did not yet know. And Simeon tells them: this baby that you are holding will suffer greatly. He will be a sign spoken against. The hearts of those who had a form of godliness but were full of darkness inside — specifically the religious leaders — will be revealed. And as a mother, Mary will suffer as she sees her son rejected and eventually killed.

This does not mean that Mary’s suffering was part of the atonement — that she is a co-redemptrix with Christ. There is one Mediator between God and man, the man Christ Jesus (1 Timothy 2:5). Simeon is speaking of the natural grief of a mother.

Simeon also prophesies that through Christ, *“the thoughts of many hearts may be revealed.”* As the Word of God shines like a light, what is in the heart of man is revealed — not to God, who already knows, but to us. We know ourselves best when we know ourselves in the light of the gospel.

## Conclusion: How to Live in Light of This

**Receive Jesus as the Consolation of Israel by faith.** Do not resist Him. He is the One who comes alongside us, who reconciles us to God.

**See Jesus as the light and glory of the world, the only Savior of mankind.** There is no other name under heaven by which we must be saved.

**Open your heart to the Word of God and the work of the Spirit** so that what is in your heart may be revealed to you — so that you might repent, believe, and follow Christ.

**Stand upon Jesus even at the cost of rejection.** He was a sign spoken against. His followers will be also. But we are built up in the faith as we hold to Christ, who is our rock, our salvation, our consolation — forever.
